speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll finish the story in 30 minutes, so we'll be ready. You can come down and watch us film the documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they're not VHS-- you're going to check them? If there was a problem, then-- No, the problem's gone now. Oh. There was a-- I'm not sure there was a problem. But I-- Can I have your pen? I was having something in my monitor--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing fine giving me the two-minute warnings, [INAUDIBLE]. That's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're ready. We're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 3. Today is February 24, 1995. Survivor Marianne Cooper. Before we get to liberation, let's go back just for a couple of minutes and tell me a little bit about the Jewish content in your home that you can remember from when you were four, five, and six, before you went into hiding. And then comment on what Jewish content, if any, was possible when you were in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My mother was a very religious woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We always celebrated Rosh Hashana, Yom Kippur, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of the High Holy Days. We also always celebrated Pesach. We always observed-- we always had Seders. So that is something that is very strong in my memory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were a very small family, so the recollection says to me that it was the immediate family. This one-- I only had this one aunt and uncle that I spoke about. And they had two daughters, who were substantially older than I was. And my mother had some aunts and uncles, so I remember celebrating with them. That was prior to '44, so that I remember a series of holiday observations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Although as I said earlier, my mother never kept kosher, but there was always observation of all of the holidays. And there was never any question about that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As I mentioned, the Germans came in March 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very little observation of anything was possible after that. I have no recollections of observing the High Holy Days in '44. There could have been some very minimal, but times were really very tough at that point. And already there was rationing, and there was already the curfew, so things were really not going very well. Obviously, we were-- once we went into hiding, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was very little observation. What I do remember, my mother lit the Shabbat candle for as long as she could while we were in our own apartment with the other two families. As long as she could do that, she did it. But of course, you couldn't buy Shabbat candles. One of the issues was that nothing Jewish would be available. I mean, they wanted to eradicate anything that had anything to do with Jews. So that issue became a non-issue because it was simply not possible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, did we know we were Jews? Yes, we did know we were Jews. My mother always had her prayer book. That's something that I have someplace hidden here in the house. I have it somewhere. That she always kept, and, and she used it. So that, that was very much part of her life. How much of it did she pass on to her children? A great deal. We can talk about it as, as time goes on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't believe during this, let's say, 10 to 12 months, there was much possibility for anything. Now remember, liberation happened in April of '45. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somewhere around there was Pesach. People really couldn't think of observing Pesach too much because people were thinking about getting food to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that year, observing it was not an issue. The following years, I have very strong recollections of having Seders. But that year, the issue was to stay alive. That was the major issue, nothing else. And whatever it took to stay alive, that's what you had to do. And that's what you had to do as a little kid. And that's what you had to do as an adult.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I said earlier, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the idea of, of being branded as a Jew-- because you were branded. That's exactly what the yellow star is. It sort of etches it in your mind for good. It's not something that you ever forget. And I think talking about the yellow Star of David as a symbol of being branded as a Jew is a very important issue. And I don't know if much was made of that as yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the most universal thing how Jews were identified. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you couldn't escape that. And, and that, that is one of the memories I have that I was-- yes, I was a Jew. I was brought up as a Jew. I knew I was a Jew. But by other people's decision, I was branded as a Jew. And whether you were a young kid or you were a teenager or you were an adult, you wore that yellow star. And that was permanently sewn onto your coat. You did not leave your apartment unless that was on you. That's a pretty strong memory to grow up with. And I tell you what that did to me. I have become a real-- if I wasn't in Hungary, I would call myself a Zionist. But I was in Hungary, so that was not an issue. I wasn't a Zionist. But the feeling, the strong feeling of being a Jew is, is something that I have come away with from all of this. And giving my children a Jewish education and joining a temple and being members of a synagogue was the way how I felt I could make sure that the idea of being a Jew continued. And that was something that meant an awful lot to me. And I know that it meant an awful lot to my children. And also, for whatever reason-- and that probably is out of, out of time to talk about that-- but I attribute the fact that they both married Jews to that. I really do attribute that, although maybe I shouldn't. I don't know. But just the fact that I, we were able to instill in them the idea of being a Jew and the importance of being a Jew. And it obviously pleases me to no end that they both married nice, nice guys and that they also happen to be Jewish. That's something that's very important to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the last days of hiding--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and liberation. What do you remember of that? And then let's go forward to today.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fighting. I remember all of a sudden the Russians appearing in this house where we were hiding. And I remember my father coming back with the Russians. And this ends up being a very, very much individual interesting story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Having-- since my father was a prisoner of Russia for five or six years, he spoke Russian. And he was a very unique individual. And he had a lot of good as well as bad experiences as a result of that. He was a translator. He was the very-- among the very, very few Hungarians who could speak to the Russians in Russian. Now obviously, for all those years between World War I and World War II, he didn't practice it. But it came back, and he was speaking to them. Another memory moving forward, once the Russians came in, let's-- just let me repeat again. I can thank the Russians for my life. The Soviet army is the army that liberated me. And that is a fact. And there is absolutely no question about that. It wasn't the American army. It wasn't the British army, the French. And that was because of Yalta, where they divided up who was going to get what part of the world. So the beginnings of the Cold War were already right there. But meanwhile, we survived, and we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do I remember? I remember several things. I remember going back to the apartment where I grew up, where we then-- if you'll recall, that was a designated Jewish house. That's what it was called. It was a designated Jewish house. And finding it totally without any windows, totally without any electricity, totally without any heat, none of that. I have very clear memories of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have very clear memories of my mother scrounging and trying to find food. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the story that I already told about the corn is very strong memory after immediately after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have another very strong memory. The memory is that for months, well into the summer, my mother wouldn't allow my father to go out on the street without me. Why was that? Because the Russians were doing the same things the Germans and the Arrow Crosses were doing, is rounding up able-bodied men and sending them to the Gulag, to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother and father decided he's been there already. He's done that once. He's not going to do that one more time. So that the assumption was that if you had a little kid with you, you were going to be safe because they will not take you away from your kid. And that's exactly what happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was never taken. And we know of people whom the Russians took away. Just think of Wallenberg, who was taken by the Russians. So that's another very, very vivid memory that I do have. I have recollections of school having started. And I have-- in that context, I went to school and finished first grade in six weeks. But we were declared having finished first grade, and we were promoted to go to second grade. Everybody just simply wanted to go on with their lives. I remember everybody was looking for their friends, who survived and who was there, whether it was Jew or Christian. That, that is something that I remember. I remembered the city, which is a gorgeous city, being in total ruins. There are-- the city used to be called Little Paris because it has the two sides of the river dividing it and the bridges connecting them. And the bridges were all destroyed. So I have recollection of this pontoon bridge that was first created so that people could cross the river from either side and how slowly the city was-- began to come back. I also remember how my father was trying to reestablish his law practice. Now, we didn't talk much about the fact how he was not allowed to practice and how all the Jews had to be reinstated as life came back by the Bar Association to be allowed to practice again. And so he was among those who got himself early reinstated. I remember the political turmoil, to some extent. There were a lot of political parties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were-- the communists were very slow in asserting themselves. I remember my sister, who has become a young woman who was dating. I have memories of her dating. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have memories of, of parties, her parties, as life was trying to return to normal in my house. I have memories--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you living in your original apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the original apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we went back, and the-- it was restored. Basically, the building was not destroyed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were no windows, and there was no electricity and none of that, but it all came back, and everything was basically put back within a few months. I remember boarded-up windows. I remember being extremely cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your father have to be reinstated? Why did the Jewish lawyers have to be reinstated? Why didn't they just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were wiped from the rosters of people because of that the law that was passed. There was a law that was passed in '43, I think, that no Jewish lawyer or Jewish doctor could be practiced. And so if you cannot practice, you're wiped from the roster of those who have passed the bar and allowed to practice in the bar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Hungarian government, after liberation, eagerly reinstate these people? Or we there problems? Do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if there were any problems. I know that he was reinstated, and he was practicing law by early, late '45, early '46. He was beginning to practice law again. So something must have gone on. Now, I was in elementary school. Came 1948-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister got married in 1948. So that's one very nice association I have with '48. On the other hand, the other association with '48 is that the communists came in. And the terror and the people's disappearance and people's deportation began again at that time. Now, you are looking at Hungarians, not-- some Jews, many non-Jews, again being uprooted, deported within the country. And I-- my parents had-- I can think of two sets of people to whom it-- I had a friend and my parents had a friend who where deported from their very comfortable environments. And all of their property is taken away and sent into the countryside, where they were given a room in a peasant's house. And that is where they were going to live. And whatever they were given to do is what they had to be doing. Now, I remember a period where in, let's say, from 1949, '50, '51, when my father was still practicing, but lawyers were, at that point, not being allowed to practice. It's not because you're a Jew or a non-Jew. It is because you're a lawyer who's supposed to be a pariah, according to the communists. And my mother living in constant fear that we are going to be deported within the country. Every time the doorbell would ring or every time something would happen, there was this fear, absolute fear, that the KGB equivalent of Hungary was at the door, and they were taking us away, which is-- not too many people have that kind of recollection. So at that point, it wasn't the fact that we were Jews. It was the fact that he was a lawyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what's happening to you? You're just racing through school in a normal way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am racing-- I am racing through school. Because the communists came in, I was not allowed to have a Jewish education. And I think that's very important. After second grade, no more Jewish education. Religion was outlawed. All religion was totally outlawed. And that was not allowed. Now, I have a very clear memory. In the early '50s, my parents buying High Holy Day tickets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that point, religion is really frowned upon. And this is not something that you do. But my parents did it as long as they could do it. And I have recollections of them being in shul and fasting on Yom Kippur and having the whole idea being held up that, yes, you are a Jew. And this is a Jewish holiday. And this is what you do. I also have recollections of family Seders, so that my parents upheld the tradition and upheld the idea that we were Jews. And if we survived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything that we did, the Holocaust, and we survived the war, then we certainly will continue as much as we possibly can, the Jewish tradition. But you are looking at someone who is a Jew, very much of a Jew, without a Jewish education. And this is also one of the reasons why it was so important to me that my children got a Jewish education. Another thing that came out of that is that I wanted my children to get a Jewish education where the fact that they were women had no problem with it. So this is why we were members and are members of a reform congregation, because as women within the reform congregation, they did everything the same thing as the boys did. And that was something that became extremely important to both of us, my husband and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Going back. Let's go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you graduated from elementary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated from elementary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Junior high.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elementary school was eight years. That was the communist system was that you went to the same school for eight years. And then I went to high school, which was, which was called the gymnasium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to gymnasium. I graduated. Went through it. And I have a couple of interesting stories from there. Graduated in June of '56. That's when I graduated from high school. Couple of the interesting stories have to do with the high school where I went to and the system under which I grew up. Remember, I grew up during the worst of the Stalin era. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I-- my father was a regular listener to Radio Free Europe and the BBC. So we-- he was always very much aware of what was going on outside of the country in the normal world. And one of the memories I have growing up under those circumstances was, shut your mouth, never say a word. Don't ever even think about talking about your father listening to Western radio. Shortwave radio is the way you got it. And don't ever talk about anything. Once you're out of this apartment, you don't talk about anything that happens in this apartment. And I have clear memories every day as I left the apartment to go to school, that was the last thing my mother said to me. Remember, don't say anything. The other interesting memory is this high school, the gymnasium that I went to, the woman who was the principal-- this is all-girls' school. I went to all-girls' schools because that's the way the system worked, both elementary and, and high school. The principal of this school was a half Jew, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] married to one of the army high mucky mucks, a major general in the army, who was not a Jew. She was brought up by Catholic nuns. So she combined in this school that she was the principal of, she combined-- we were called red nuns. Anybody who graduated from school under her principalship was called red nuns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were under extremely strict [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ethical requirements. But we were also under-- which was combined with the communist ethics. So it created a very interesting environment to grow up. You basically have to understand that I had two lives. One life was in school, and the other life was outside of school. To bring in a Jewish story, I must've been, I think, a sophomore in high school. And it was Yom Kippur. And I was fasting. And this lady-- but nobody was supposed to know that I was in school on Yom Kippur fasting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this lady-- I, I actually remember this-- who was a half Jew and knew very well that this was Yom Kippur-- I was standing in a corner talking to my friends. And she came around and said where's your lunch, knowing very well where my lunch was.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, I didn't see you in the lunch room. You didn't eat. So I said, I wasn't hungry. And she said to me, you're not eating because it's a Jewish holiday. And I said, what if I'm not? So she says, there's not supposed to be any religious observation. I have very clear memories of that. So I really think I bring a very mixed-up kind of a background to everything that I have ended up doing and being. Now, what else do I remember? I remember in '52 my father was no longer able to maintain a private law practice. So somewhere in '53 or so, he joined a newly formed cooperative, which could be viewed as a law firm, which is what they were doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: A whole bunch of lawyers were getting together and forming a cooperative, hoping that as a group, they were all going to make a living, and they were all going to survive. So I remember him going from a private, single attorney into this group practice, which totally changed everybody's life around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I remember that. I remember, as I mentioned, my sister got married. And I remember her having a couple of kids being born. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember them working very hard, everybody working very hard, trying to make a living. That was a very major issue, trying to make enough so that you can maintain some semblance of, of a decent standard of living. Life was not easy. Life was not easy by any means.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in '56? And how did you get to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I graduated from high school in '56. I did not get into college. In fact, I just found the papers where I was notified that I did not get into the, into the college. And the reason was, is, was very interesting. The formal reason-- I wanted to go to law school. And the formal reason was-- I passed all the tests with flying colors-- there was not enough room to take all the qualified applicants. And as my father learned, the reason I didn't get in is because he was a lawyer from the old times, and they didn't want children of lawyers of that capitalistic generation to go to law school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're looking at someone who did not get into college, was totally devastated, absolutely devastated. I also lost my father in '56, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] which is due to, as we were trying to figure out, being in Siberia, surviving two world wars, and being a heavy smoker was just more than his body could take. So he died in '56 at the age of 58.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything that I remember, the Hungarian revolution started, which was started by, primarily, students. Here goes another turmoil. Here goes another time of curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: You cannot go out. You are limited in what you can do. There is no food again. I mean, it's deja vu all over again. There's an incredible amount of street fighting on the-- everywhere. There are tanks rolling in. They have-- for a few days, everybody's successful. Then the Russian tanks roll in, and everything is over. And they are trying to reassert themselves as the rulers with their Hungarian counterparts who were going with them. There was a lot of political turmoil. The people who were doing the-- during the revolution, who became prime minister, was killed by the new regime, by a mock trial. Was found guilty and shot. A whole lot of other people who were partaking in that government were all shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, as the revolution commenced, the borders opened. Now what were the borders? The western border is Austria. And also the other border was Yugoslavia. Yugoslavia separated itself from the Russians under Tito. And Tito ran his own country the way he wanted to run it. So the borders opened, and people started to leave. They didn't want to live like this. They didn't want to live under these conditions. The persecution was going to come back. The persecution was there. The persecution is coming back. Something has to change.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and her family, two babies, and my brother-in-law, decided they were leaving. Now, remember, at that point, my father is gone already, and it's my mother and I. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they are leaving. They're packing up, and they are taking the kids and, and a backpack, each one of them. And through very dubious means, they're leaving the country. They are making my mother promise that she will follow with me. Now I am 18. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we have decided, the two of us-- here is again, the extremely strong woman who is 50, 51, something like that, who decides that she cannot have her family separated. And she complies. And we decide we are going to leave now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll pick up--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the next tape with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
